notes.anchor: the Vocontii were close at hand, through whose territory the road lay open to me
notes.note: The Vocontii were a Gallic people of the southern pre-Alps (around modern Vaison and Die), allied to Rome and friendly to Plancus; their loyal territory secured his line of retreat from Lepidus and Antony.

notes.anchor: joined himself to Antony on 29 May
notes.note: Lepidus's army went over to Antony at the Argenteus on 29 May 43 BC (Latin a.d. iiii K. Iun.), a decisive defection that united the two against the Senate and forced Plancus to withdraw across the Isère.

notes.anchor: the hands he should more justly have armed against Lepidus's destruction he turned upon himself
notes.note: Laterensis, who had vouched for Lepidus's loyalty, attempted suicide on discovering the betrayal; Plancus reports he survived the attempt but was uncertain of his recovery (he in fact died of the wound).

notes.anchor: because C. Catius Vestinus, a military tribune sent by Antony to him with a letter, I had intercepted
notes.note: A tribunus militum is a senior legionary officer; Plancus's interception of Antony's courier C. Catius Vestinus, and his treating the man as an enemy, was one of the provocations that turned Antony's and Lepidus's troops against him.

notes.anchor: Let Caesar come with the forces he has, which are very strong
notes.note: "Caesar" here is the young Octavian (Caesar's heir), then commanding the strongest body of troops in Italy; Plancus's plea for him or his army to be sent shows how much the Senate's cause now depended on the nineteen-year-old.

notes.anchor: from Cularo in the territory of the Allobroges
notes.note: Cularo (modern Grenoble), a town on the Isère in the country of the Allobroges, was Plancus's base after his retreat; the Allobroges were the powerful Gallic tribe between the Rhone, Isère, and Alps.
